---
title: "Concepts Explained"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## How to use this page

This appendix is for readers who would like a plain-language
walkthrough of the technical concepts the rest of the report
relies on. Each entry is short — roughly two or three minutes of
reading — and is written for someone who has not seen the concept
before.

The main text of the report links here. If you hit a term in the
Executive Summary or Introduction that doesn't quite resolve,
click through; the entry will explain it, and you can return to
where you were.

This page complements the **Glossary & Metrics** reference (which
is shorter and organised for quick lookup). Use the glossary when
you want a one-line definition; use this page when you want to
understand the concept.
:::

---

## 1. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) {#concept-rag}

**Retrieval-Augmented Generation**, usually written **RAG**, is
the pattern of giving a language model the relevant documents
*alongside* the user's question, rather than expecting the model
to answer from its training data.

A language model on its own has read a great deal of text during
training, but its knowledge is frozen at a point in time and is
not specific to any one organisation. Ask such a model "what is
Munich Re's 2024 thermal coal policy?" and the answer will
typically be plausible-sounding, possibly out of date, and not
verifiable against any specific source.

The RAG pattern fixes this with a two-step flow. First, the
system *retrieves* relevant passages from a document collection
that the model didn't necessarily see during training — in
Cedant's case, the indexed corpus of regulatory and corporate
documents. Second, the system *generates* an answer using those
retrieved passages as the source material. The model is given
both the question and the passages; the model writes its answer
based on what the passages actually say.

A useful analogy: a research assistant who has memorised a great
deal of general knowledge can write a passable summary from
memory, but if you want them to cite a specific clause of a
specific contract, you hand them the contract first. RAG is the
software version of handing the model the contract first.

The pattern matters in reinsurance because the documents that
matter most — internal policies, recent regulatory updates,
broker submissions — are precisely the documents a general-purpose
model has not been trained on.

---

## 2. Hybrid Retrieval (Semantic + Keyword) {#concept-hybrid-retrieval}

When Cedant goes looking for the relevant passages in the
document collection, it uses **two** search methods at the same
time and combines the results.

**Keyword search** (the technical name is BM25) looks for
documents that contain the same words as the query. It is
excellent at finding exact matches for named entities —
regulation identifiers ("SS5/25"), company names ("Munich Re"),
specific defined terms ("ORSA"). It is less good when the user
phrases the question one way and the document phrases the answer
another way ("greenhouse gas exposure" vs "GHG emissions
profile").

**Semantic search** (the technical name is dense embedding
retrieval) looks for documents that *mean* the same thing as the
query, even when they use different words. It is excellent at
catching paraphrases and conceptual matches. It is less good at
exact named-entity recall — if you ask for "paragraph 4.124"
specifically, semantic search may surface paragraphs that *talk
about* the same topic but are not the one you wanted.

The two methods catch what the other misses. Using both — and
combining their results with a small mathematical trick called
Reciprocal Rank Fusion — produces noticeably better retrieval
than either alone. This is what *hybrid retrieval* means.

For reinsurance documents specifically, the case for hybrid
retrieval is strong: regulatory text is dense with named entities
(supervisory statements, defined terms, paragraph numbers), while
the surrounding descriptive prose is exactly the territory where
semantic search dominates.

---

## 3. The Citation Contract {#concept-citation-contract}

Cedant makes an explicit commitment to a property called the
**citation contract**: every factual claim in an answer must be
paired with a citation to a specific paragraph in a specific
document, and the citation must resolve to a real source the
system actually used.

The contract is enforced *structurally*, which is the load-bearing
distinction. A language model can be *told* in its instructions
to cite its sources — and might or might not actually do so.
"Structurally enforced" means the system does not rely on the
model's good behaviour. It checks.

Specifically: every citation the model emits is captured by a
parser, and each captured citation is checked against the
collection of paragraphs the system actually showed to the model.
A citation that points to something the model never saw is flagged
as a **hallucinated citation** and is surfaced explicitly in the
user interface (as a red badge) rather than silently allowed
through.

The contract matters because, in this domain, an incorrect
citation is worse than no citation at all. An underwriter relying
on a fabricated citation in a committee paper has acquired an
auditable error rather than a vague impression. A loose citation
discipline — even one that *usually* gets it right — is not safe
to deploy in a high-consequence workflow.

The citation contract is what allows the system to be useful in
that workflow at all.

---

## 4. The Refusal Contract {#concept-refusal-contract}

The companion to the citation contract is the **refusal
contract**: when the document collection does not contain enough
information to answer the question, the system must refuse to
answer, rather than guess.

The refusal contract is implemented as an *exact phrase*. The
system commits to emitting the words

> *"I cannot answer this from the provided sources."*

— and nothing else — when retrieval surfaces no relevant
documents, or when the model determines the retrieved documents
do not contain the answer. A separate detector in the code looks
for this exact phrase and uses it as the signal that the system
refused.

The strictness of the exact-phrase rule is deliberate. A loose
refusal detector — one that accepts "I don't quite know" or "I
can't be sure" or "the documents don't seem to cover this" —
introduces ambiguity into something that needs to be unambiguous.
The system must either refuse, or answer with citations; there is
no middle ground.

This matters most in the hardest case: questions where the
document collection *does* discuss the topic but does not contain
the specific detail being asked for. A language model is most
tempted to confabulate exactly here. The refusal contract is what
keeps the system honest in those cases — and the evaluation
demonstrates the contract holds (every refusal question in the
benchmark, across every test cell, is refused correctly).

---

## 5. Hallucination {#concept-hallucination}

In the context of language models, **hallucination** refers to
content that the model generates which is not true, not
supported, or not consistent with its source material — but is
phrased confidently as if it were.

The classic example is a model that, asked for the citation of a
court case, produces a confidently-written reference to a case
that does not exist. The language is fluent; the content is
fabricated. Hallucinations are dangerous precisely because they
do not signal their own untrustworthiness.

In Cedant, hallucination most commonly refers to **hallucinated
citations** — citations that look syntactically valid and point
to plausible-looking chunk identifiers, but whose identifiers do
not match any chunk the system actually retrieved. A hallucinated
citation looks correct at a glance and only fails when you try to
look up the source.

Cedant's structural citation contract is designed specifically to
catch this failure mode. Every citation is checked; every
hallucinated citation is partitioned out and surfaced to the user
(rather than passed through silently). The evaluation tracks the
count of hallucinated citations per model — the
production-default model produces zero across the full
44-question answerable test set.

Note that hallucination, in this sense, is a *technical* finding
about citation validity. It does not measure whether the prose
itself is semantically correct — a model could in principle write
prose that misrepresents what its (validly-cited) source actually
says. The report is careful about this distinction; semantic
correctness is a separate problem, recorded as future work.

---

## 6. Local-First Execution {#concept-local-first}

Cedant runs entirely on the operator's own machine — a single
laptop. There are no API calls to remote services at any stage of
the pipeline, including the evaluation. This property is called
**local-first** execution.

What this means in practice: the search index, the language model
weights, and all inference happen on the user's hardware. Document
content never leaves the machine. The system can run without an
internet connection (after the model weights have been downloaded
once).

What it costs: the system is limited to language models that can
run on local hardware, which means it cannot access the very
largest cloud-hosted models. The production-default model
(Gemma 4 31B IT) is substantial but not the largest available; a
hosted alternative could in principle produce different results.
The system is also currently tied to Apple Silicon hardware (the
M-series Mac chips), since the model-serving stack is built on
Apple's MLX framework.

What it buys: data sovereignty, which is the central point. For
many reinsurers, the documents that matter most — internal
underwriting policies, broker submissions under non-disclosure
agreements, internal pricing rationale — cannot legally or
practically be sent to a third-party API. Local-first execution
is not an architectural preference; it is the only way to make
the system usable in that environment at all.

A secondary benefit is auditability: the same query against the
same documents produces the same answer, which matters for
post-hoc verification of what the system told a particular
underwriter on a particular date.

---

## 7. Chunks {#concept-chunks}

When Cedant indexes a document, it does not store the document as
a single object. It splits the document into smaller pieces
called **chunks** — typically a few hundred to about fifteen
hundred words each — and stores each chunk separately.

There are two reasons for the split. First, language models can
only process a limited amount of text at once (the *context
window*); whole documents are often too large. Second, retrieval
works better at the level of focused passages than at the level of
whole documents — a question about thermal coal policy is best
answered by surfacing the specific thermal-coal paragraph, not by
returning the entire 80-page sustainability report.

Every chunk has a unique identifier. For example:

```
pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis
```

This combines the source document (`pra_ss5-25_climate`), a
sequence number (`0027`), and a short slug describing the section
(`role-of-scenario-analysis`). When the language model emits a
citation, it cites the chunk identifier; the system then verifies
that the identifier corresponds to a real chunk that was actually
retrieved.

The chunking algorithm in Cedant is a small piece of engineering
in its own right (described in Section 3 and decision log entry
D008). It respects document structure where possible — section
headings, numbered paragraphs — rather than splitting at fixed
character boundaries, because a citation should land on a
semantically coherent passage rather than on an arbitrary slice.

---

## 8. Falsification Criterion {#concept-falsification}

A **falsification criterion** is a pre-stated rule specifying
what evidence would force a claim to be retracted or weakened.

The principle is methodological. Without a pre-stated criterion,
it is too easy to rationalise inconvenient evidence after the
fact — "of course we didn't expect that subset to behave that
way" — without any record of what the original expectation
actually was. A criterion stated *before* the data arrives makes
the retraction visible, and the act of retracting honest.

Cedant uses falsification criteria most prominently in the
evaluation. The Day-2 hypothesis was that one of the two
candidate models had structurally weaker citation discipline than
the other, in a way that no prompt change could close. The
criterion, stated before the evaluation ran, was: *if a revised
prompt closes the gap to within ten percentage points on a key
citation metric, the structural-difference claim is retracted.*

When the evaluation ran, the gap closed to 3.2 percentage points
— well inside the criterion. The claim was retracted on the
record, with timestamp and commit hash, in the project journal.

The discipline is not unique to this project — it is borrowed
from a long tradition in scientific methodology — but it is
unusual enough in software work that it deserves explicit naming.
A report that names what evidence would change its conclusions is
a report that has thought about the difference between *believing
something* and *knowing it*.

---

## 9. The N=70 Benchmark {#concept-benchmark}

A **benchmark** in this context is a fixed set of test questions,
each with a known correct answer, used to measure how well the
system performs. The same set is used for every test run, so
results across different models or different system versions can
be compared directly.

Cedant's benchmark contains **seventy questions**, hand-written
by the project author after reading the indexed corpus. Each
question is one of two types:

- **Answerable questions** (44 of the 70). For these, the author
  has identified one or more specific paragraphs in the corpus —
  the *gold chunks* — that contain the answer. The system is
  scored on whether it cites those gold chunks.
- **Refusal questions** (26 of the 70). For these, the author
  has verified that the corpus does *not* contain the answer.
  The system is scored on whether it correctly refuses.

The refusal questions are themselves partitioned into three
categories of increasing subtlety: clearly out-of-scope questions,
questions where the topic *is* discussed in the corpus but the
specific detail is not, and questions that assume a false premise.
The hardest of these is the middle category — language models are
most tempted to confabulate exactly when they have *some* relevant
material to work from but not enough.

Seventy questions is small by machine-learning benchmark
standards. The trade-off is deliberate: a smaller benchmark can
be inspected by hand, the gold answers can be verified
individually, and failures can be diagnosed one question at a
time rather than as statistical aggregates. A larger benchmark
would lose this property, which would matter more than the
statistical-power gain at this stage of the project.

---

## 10. LLM-as-Judge (and Why This Report Does Not Use It) {#concept-llm-as-judge}

**LLM-as-judge** is a pattern in language-model evaluation where a
*second* language model is used to score the output of the
*first*. The judge model is given the question, the answer, and
the source material, and is asked: does this answer correctly
represent what the sources say?

The pattern is increasingly common in published evaluations
because it can measure something that simpler scorers cannot —
whether the model's *prose* accurately reflects its *sources*,
not just whether the citations point to the right place.

Cedant's evaluation **does not** currently use LLM-as-judge. The
decision is deliberate and is recorded in the open-questions log.
Three reasons:

- A judge model adds another uncalibrated source of error. If the
  judge model disagrees with a human reviewer, debugging the
  disagreement is non-trivial. At this stage of the project, the
  team needs a scorer whose behaviour is fully understood.
- A judge model adds latency to every test run, which matters
  because the evaluation is run often and on a fixed time
  budget.
- The structural scorer Cedant uses — which checks whether the
  cited chunks match the expected gold chunks — is honest about
  what it does not measure. Adding LLM-as-judge would change the
  honesty profile; the report would have to be careful to
  distinguish "structural correctness" from "semantic
  correctness" in every results table.

What the report explicitly *does not claim*, in the absence of an
LLM-as-judge layer, is that the model's prose is semantically
correct. The report claims only that citations resolve to the
right chunks. This is a *structural proxy* for semantic
correctness, not semantic correctness itself.

Adding LLM-as-judge is one of the explicit items in the planned
next phase of work.

---

## Where to go from here

Each concept above is referenced from the main report. If you
arrived here from a section link, the back button will return you
to where you were reading. If you arrived here directly, the
**Executive Summary** is the natural place to start; the report is
organised so that a reader can finish that section knowing what
the system does, what it does not do, and where the load-bearing
evidence lives.

For shorter reference-style lookups (acronyms, metric names,
subset partitions), see the **Glossary & Metrics** page.
